original_hypothesis.title: Supply/borrow index uint128 casting may brick assets under extreme rate model outputs or long inactivity windows
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: InterestLogic stores supplyIndex and borrowIndex as uint128 and uses SafeCastUpgradeable.toUint128() when updating. Under extreme borrowRate/supplyRate values (e.g., misconfigured/buggy interest rate model) or very long time deltas, next indexes can exceed uint128 max, causing permanent reverts on updateInterestIndexs for the asset, effectively bricking all operations touching that asset.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While reaching uint128 max requires very large growth from 1e27, the rate model is an external contract per group (GroupData.rateModel) and can be upgraded/configured. A single misconfiguration can cause non-recoverable DoS for that asset without a migration path shown here.
deployment_assumptions: Admin configures an interest rate model that can return extremely high rates or behaves unexpectedly
Asset experiences long periods without interactions, increasing (block.timestamp - lastUpdateTimestamp)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: GroupData.rateModel is externally set via configuration (Configurator not shown). InterestLogic.updateInterestRates calls rateModel.calculateGroupBorrowRate and stores to uint128.

proof_of_signal.pattern: nextSupplyIndex/nextBorrowIndex cast via toUint128(); overflow reverts and blocks further operations

preconditions: borrowRate/supplyRate becomes very large OR time delta becomes very large
An operation triggers updateInterestIndexs for the asset
No administrative recovery mechanism exists to reset indexes safely

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Narrowing cast of critical accumulator state
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Permanent asset-level DoS and protocol insolvency risk if users cannot repay/withdraw
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No upper-bound checks or circuit-breakers on rate outputs/time delta before casting

analysis_questions: What are realistic maximum outputs from DefaultInterestRateModel and can they be admin-upgraded?
Is there any admin function to pause an asset or change rateModel to recover, given updates still revert?
Could a malicious rateModel be installed by a compromised admin (operational) or via config bug?
validation_requirements: Compute worst-case index growth under max expected rates and inactivity windows
Unit tests with mocked IRM returning extreme rates to see if/when cast reverts
Review configurator/admin controls for rateModel upgrades and emergency remediation

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Through governance misconfiguration or a buggy IRM upgrade, borrowRate spikes and nextBorrowIndex exceeds uint128 max. Subsequent user actions (repay/liquidate/withdraw) revert when calling updateInterestIndexs, freezing the market and potentially locking funds.
prerequisites: Misconfigured or buggy interest rate model, or extreme time delta

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Market freeze and inability to repay/withdraw, potentially leading to cascading insolvency
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the affected asset across the pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Likely requires upgrade and state migration; difficult if all state-changing paths revert

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No stress tests for index overflow boundaries

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor index values approaching uint128 max and abnormal rate outputs

validation_result.mechanism: uint128 narrowing cast on monotonically increasing accumulator
validation_result.impact_summary: Supply/borrow index overflow via toUint128() cast would permanently brick all operations on the affected asset with no recovery path shown in code.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis identifies a real code defect in InterestLogic.sol. Both `_updateSupplyIndex` and `_updateBorrowIndex` compute a new index value as uint256 and then cast it to uint128 via SafeCastUpgradeable.toUint128(). If this cast overflows, it reverts permanently because every user-facing operation (deposit, withdraw, borrow, repay, liquidate) calls `updateInterestIndexs()` as its first step. There is no cap on the rate model output (beyond uint128 for the rate itself, which is ~3.4e38 in Ray), no cap on the time delta, no circuit breaker, and no admin function to manually reset indices.

Starting from RAY (1e27), the index can grow by a factor of ~3.4e11 before overflowing uint128. With the deployed DefaultInterestRateModel at max utilization (115% APY), this would take approximately 190 years of continuous max-rate compounding—impractical with current configuration. However, the rate model is externally configurable per group (GroupData.rateModel), and the code imposes no upper bound on what the rate model can return. A misconfigured, upgraded, or malicious rate model returning very high rates could cause overflow within a single block.

Even with sane rate models, the borrowRate stored as uint128 allows values up to ~3.4e38. If such a rate were stored (which would pass the toUint128 cast in updateInterestRates), a single second of elapsed time would produce a compound interest factor that, multiplied by the index, would exceed uint128 max. The lack of validation on rate model outputs before storage creates a path to permanent asset bricking.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows the narrowing cast pattern. In `_updateSupplyIndex`, `nextSupplyIndex.toUint128()` is used. In `_updateBorrowIndex`, `nextBorrowIndex.toUint128()` is used. Both receive uint256 values that can exceed uint128 max under certain conditions. The SafeCastUpgradeable.toUint128() function reverts on overflow. Since `updateInterestIndexs` is called at the start of virtually every protocol operation, a revert here blocks all functionality for the asset.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: nextSupplyIndex is computed as uint256 and cast to uint128 via toUint128(). If the supply rate is very high or the time delta is very large, this cast will revert, permanently blocking all operations on this asset.

nextBorrowIndex uses compounded interest which grows faster than linear. Combined with a high stored borrowRate (up to uint128 max ~3.4e38) and any time delta, the result can exceed uint128 max, causing permanent revert.

The rate from the external rate model is only bounded by uint128 max (~3.4e38 in Ray). No sanity check ensures the rate is within reasonable bounds (e.g., < 1000% APY). A uint128-max rate would cause index overflow in a single second of elapsed time.

calling_sequence: assetData.supplyIndex = nextSupplyIndex.toUint128() — REVERTS if > uint128 max
updateInterestIndexs calls _updateBorrowIndex(assetData, groupData) for each group
groupData.borrowIndex = nextBorrowIndex.toUint128() — REVERTS if > uint128 max
All subsequent operations on this asset permanently fail
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Every user-facing operation calls updateInterestIndexs at the start. If the index computation overflows uint128, the toUint128() safe cast reverts. Since there is no way to update the index without going through this function, and no admin function to bypass it or manually set the index, the asset becomes permanently bricked. Users cannot withdraw, repay, or liquidate.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Admin configures an interest rate model that can return extremely high rates or behaves unexpectedly
Asset experiences long periods without interactions, increasing (block.timestamp - lastUpdateTimestamp)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: InitConfigPool.s.sol shows DefaultInterestRateModel instances with max slope2 of 100% (1e27 Ray) and max base rate of 10%. The maximum combined rate at full utilization would be ~115% APY. At this rate, index overflow would take ~190 years. However, rate models are set via configurator.addAssetGroup() and can be changed. The Configurator contract is not fully provided, but the architecture shows rateModel is an admin-configurable address per group. No deployment script constrains future rate model changes.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The most realistic scenario involves admin misconfiguration or a buggy IRM upgrade. If a rate model is deployed that returns rates near uint128 max (e.g., due to a bug in calculation), the borrowRate stored would be enormous. On the next block's interaction, _updateBorrowIndex would compute cumulatedBorrowInterest with this extreme rate and even 1 second of elapsed time, producing a compound factor >> 1e27. Multiplied by the existing borrowIndex (>= 1e27), the result easily exceeds uint128 max. The toUint128() cast reverts, and all operations on the asset are permanently bricked. Users with deposits cannot withdraw, borrowers cannot repay, and positions cannot be liquidated. An even simpler scenario: an asset with moderately high rates (e.g., 500% APY) that goes unused for 50+ years would also trigger overflow.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If triggered, this causes permanent denial of service for all operations on the affected asset. Users cannot withdraw funds, borrowers cannot repay debts, and liquidations cannot proceed. This locks all deposited funds and creates permanently bad debt. Recovery would require a contract upgrade and state migration, which is complex given the shared storage architecture. The severity is MEDIUM because it requires admin misconfiguration or extremely long inactivity periods with current deployed IRMs, but the impact if triggered is HIGH (permanent fund lockup).
recommended_mitigations: Add an upper bound check on rate model outputs in updateInterestRates (e.g., require rate <= MAX_REASONABLE_RATE where MAX_REASONABLE_RATE is something like 10 * RAY for 1000% APY)
Add saturation logic instead of revert: if nextIndex exceeds uint128 max, cap it at uint128 max rather than reverting, or use uint256 for indices
Add a circuit breaker / emergency admin function to pause interest accrual and manually set indices for an asset
Cap the effective time delta in updateInterestIndexs to a maximum reasonable value (e.g., 365 days)
Consider using uint256 for supplyIndex and borrowIndex to eliminate the overflow risk entirely
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock IInterestRateModel that returns a very high rate (e.g., 1e35). 2. Configure an asset group with this rate model. 3. Have a user deposit and borrow. 4. Advance block.timestamp by a small amount (e.g., 1 day). 5. Attempt any operation on the asset — expect permanent revert. 6. Verify no admin function can recover the asset. Alternative: Use a reasonable rate model (100% APY), advance time by 200 years using vm.warp, and verify overflow.
uncertainties: The Configurator contract is not provided — it may contain rate bounds that limit IRM outputs
There may be off-chain monitoring or governance processes that prevent extreme rate model configurations
The DefaultInterestRateModel internals are not provided — it may have its own output caps
The probability of an asset going uninteracted for 190+ years with current IRMs is effectively zero
additional_testing_needed: Review DefaultInterestRateModel.calculateGroupBorrowRate to verify maximum possible output
Review Configurator contract for any rate model validation during configuration
Fuzz test with various rate model outputs and time deltas to find exact overflow boundaries
Verify whether contract upgrade path exists that could reset indices without going through updateInterestIndexs
